title: The Fifth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## July 5, 2020 | Preacher: Richard Lawson

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon fell on the Fifth Sunday after Pentecost in Ordinary Time, also known as Proper 9 in the Revised Common Lectionary. The primary readings for this Sunday were likely Genesis 24:34-38, 42-49, 58-59 (the servant finding a bride for Isaac) and Matthew 11:16-19, 25-30 (Jesus's invitation to come to him for rest). The church was well into the long green season of Pentecost, a period focused on the Holy Spirit's work in the world and Christian living in response to God's grace.

---

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was navigating profound internal divisions in summer 2020 regarding sexuality and marriage, with General Convention scheduled for July (later postponed to August due to COVID-19). The Diocese of Colorado, which includes Saint John's Cathedral in Denver, was preparing for a special convention session; the diocese had been grappling with questions about LGBTQ+ inclusion and canonical interpretation. Richard Lawson, as a preacher at the cathedral, was likely aware of ongoing tensions within the Anglican Communion regarding the Lambeth Conference (also postponed from 2020 to 2022), which had become a flashpoint for conservative and progressive disagreements.

---

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing escalating racial justice protests following George Floyd's death on May 25, with multiple demonstrations occurring weekly throughout June and into early July in downtown Denver, the Capitol Hill neighborhood, and other areas. Colorado had begun phase two of reopening from COVID-19 lockdowns in late June, but Denver faced growing case numbers and hospital capacity concerns as the summer progressed. The week before this sermon saw continued protests, police reform discussions at city council meetings, and ongoing debate about statues and monuments in the Denver area (particularly the Kit Carson statue near the Capitol).

---

NATIONAL NEWS

The United States was in the grip of the Black Lives Matter movement's largest sustained wave of protests since George Floyd's death, with demonstrations occurring in hundreds of cities; President Trump had threatened federal military intervention while some governors mobilized National Guard units. COVID-19 cases were surging dramatically in Southern and Western states, including Colorado, forcing some states to reverse reopening plans and reinstate restrictions. The Senate was investigating Russian interference in the 2016 election and potential 2020 interference, with the Senate Intelligence Committee releasing reports on Russian disinformation campaigns, while President Trump faced criticism for alleged bounties on U.S. troops in Afghanistan.

---

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The United Kingdom was preparing for major economic disruption as Brexit negotiations continued without a trade deal agreement in place, with the July 1 deadline for extending negotiations approaching. Hong Kong was reeling from the imposition of China's National Security Law on July 1, just days before this sermon, which dramatically curtailed freedoms and sparked international condemnation and concern for Hong Kong's autonomy. The COVID-19 pandemic continued escalating globally, particularly in Brazil and India, while European countries debated reopening borders and vaccine development efforts accelerated.

---

CULTURAL MOMENT

American society was experiencing acute social division and grief, with the summer of 2020 marked by existential questions about systemic racism, police violence, and national identity in the wake of George Floyd's death and subsequent protests. The pandemic had created a surreal backdrop of death, isolation, and economic disruption, with many churches still closed or operating at limited capacity; Saint John's Cathedral likely had restrictions on in-person worship and was exploring hybrid or outdoor services. The cultural mood was one of urgency, anger, hope, and exhaustion simultaneously—a moment when many Americans felt they were witnessing historical reckoning, and churches faced pressure to speak prophetically on issues of justice while also providing pastoral care during unprecedented loss.
